g.data-narration: Welcome to ARM Exception Handling. Today we explore IRQ and FIQ, essential concepts for embedded systems programming.
g.text: IRQ and FIQ Exceptions in ARM Microcontrollers

g.data-narration: Exceptions are events that disrupt normal program flow, requiring the processor to execute special handling code for urgent tasks.
g.text: Exceptions are events that disrupt normal program flow, requiring the processor to
take special action. In ARM, they include Reset, SWI, IRQ, FIQ, and Abort types.

g.data-narration: Interrupt-driven programming enables efficient peripheral handling without wasteful polling, allowing real-time response to events.
IRQ is the standard interrupt for general-purpose peripherals like timers, UART, and keyboards with moderate latency requirements.
FIQ provides high-priority, low-latency interrupts with dedicated registers for time-critical operations like high-speed data transfer.
CPSR contains interrupt mask bits (I and F) and processor mode bits. SPSR saves CPSR during exception handling for restoration.
The exception vector table maps exception types to handler addresses. FIQ is placed last for direct handler execution without branch.
g.text: FIQ placed last enables direct handler execution from vector without branch instruction

g.data-narration: Interrupt latency is the time from interrupt occurrence to handler start. FIQ has lower latency due to dedicated registers and vector placement.
g.text: • Last vector position allows direct execution

g.data-narration: This IRQ handler example shows register saving, interrupt checking, service routine execution, and proper return mechanism using LDMFD.
g.text: LDMFD sp!, {r0-r12, pc}^ ; Restore and return
Key: Must save all registers (r0-r12) to stack since FIQ has dedicated registers

g.data-narration: FIQ handler is simpler due to banked registers r8-r14. No register saving needed, enabling faster execution for time-critical tasks.
g.text: ; r8-r14 are banked - no saving needed!
SUBS pc, lr, #4 ; Return from FIQ
★ Advantage: Banked registers eliminate save/restore overhead - Lower latency

g.data-narration: Modern Cortex-M processors use NVIC for vectored interrupts with priority levels, unlike traditional ARM7's vector table approach.
g.text: - ARM7: Manual priority handling, software-determined vectors
- Cortex-M: Hardware vector fetch, configurable priority levels (0-255), automatic stacking

g.data-narration: Key differences: FIQ has higher priority, dedicated registers, cannot be interrupted by IRQ, and lower latency than IRQ.
In summary: IRQ for standard peripherals with moderate latency, FIQ for time-critical high-speed operations requiring minimal latency.
g.text: IRQ - Standard peripherals (Timer, UART, Keyboard) | FIQ - Time-critical (High-speed data transfer, Real-time control)
Remember: FIQ has dedicated registers (r8-r14), highest priority, and lowest latency
